landing.title:
Visualize Tech,
Master the Stack
landing.subtitle: Interactive 3D visualizations that make complex tech concepts intuitive. See how frameworks, architectures, and design patterns actually work — from the inside.

nextjs.title: Fullstack Next.js
nextjs.description: Master Next.js architecture with interactive 3D visualizations. Understand System Design, Monorepo, SOLID Principles, and Tailwind CSS from the inside out.

systemDesign.title: System Design Overview
systemDesign.description: Visualize the complete Next.js request lifecycle — from client request through middleware, routing, server components, API layer, to database and back.

intro.title: The Big Picture
intro.content: A Next.js application is a full-stack system. Let's trace a single request through every layer to understand how it all connects.

client.title: Client Request
client.content: Everything starts with a user action in the browser. A click, a navigation, or a form submission creates an HTTP request that travels to the server.

middleware.title: Middleware Layer
middleware.content: Before reaching any page, the request passes through Middleware. Here you can redirect, rewrite URLs, set headers, or check authentication — all at the edge.

routing.title: App Router
routing.content: Next.js uses file-system routing. The App Router maps the URL path to a file in your /app directory. Each folder becomes a route segment.

serverComponent.title: Server Components
serverComponent.content: By default, components in Next.js are Server Components. They render on the server, can directly access databases and APIs, and send only HTML to the client.

apiLayer.title: API Layer
apiLayer.content: Route Handlers (route.ts) and Server Actions provide the API layer. They handle data mutations, external API calls, and business logic.

database.title: Database
database.content: The data layer connects to your database through an ORM like Prisma or Drizzle. Queries run on the server, keeping your database credentials secure.

response.title: Response & Streaming
response.content: The server streams the rendered HTML back to the client. React hydrates the page, making it interactive. Suspense boundaries enable progressive loading.

react.title: ReactJS
react.description: Master React from fundamentals to advanced patterns with interactive 3D visualizations. Understand component trees, hooks lifecycle, state management, and rendering pipeline.

componentBasics.title: Component Basics
componentBasics.description: Visualize React's component tree — how JSX becomes components, props flow down, and composition creates powerful UIs.

intro.title: The Component Tree
intro.content: Every React app is a tree of components. The root App component branches into child components, each responsible for a piece of the UI.

app.title: Root Component
app.content: The App component is the root of your tree. It's the entry point that React renders first. Everything else lives inside it.

children.title: Child Components
children.content: App splits into Header, Main, and Footer. Each is an independent component that receives data through props and manages its own rendering.

headerChildren.title: Composition: Header
headerChildren.content: Header composes Nav and Logo. This is composition — building complex UIs by combining simple, focused components together.

mainChildren.title: Composition: Main Content
mainChildren.content: Main contains ProductList and Sidebar. Components can be reused — the same ProductList could appear on different pages.

listItems.title: Lists & Keys
listItems.content: ProductList renders multiple ProductCard components from an array. Each card needs a unique 'key' prop so React can track them efficiently.

deepNesting.title: Props Drilling
deepNesting.content: ProductCard contains Button and Price. Data flows DOWN through props. When nesting gets deep, you might need Context or state management.

fullTree.title: The Full Picture
fullTree.content: The complete component tree shows how React builds complex UIs from simple pieces. Each component is isolated, reusable, and testable.

hooks.title: React Hooks
hooks.description: Understand the lifecycle of useState, useEffect, and useRef — how state triggers re-renders and side effects run after paint.

intro.title: Hooks Overview
intro.content: Hooks let function components use state and side effects. They run in order, every render, and must follow the Rules of Hooks.

component.title: The Component
component.content: A function component is just a function that returns JSX. Hooks attach superpowers to it — state, effects, refs, and more.

useState.title: useState
useState.content: useState gives your component memory. It returns [value, setter]. When you call the setter, React schedules a re-render with the new value.

stateUpdate.title: State Update Cycle
stateUpdate.content: Calling setState doesn't update immediately. React batches updates, re-renders the component, and produces new JSX with the updated state.

useEffect.title: useEffect
useEffect.content: useEffect runs AFTER the component renders. It's for side effects: fetching data, subscriptions, or DOM manipulation.

effectLifecycle.title: Effect Lifecycle
effectLifecycle.content: Effects can return a cleanup function that runs before the next effect or on unmount. This prevents memory leaks from subscriptions.

useRef.title: useRef
useRef.content: useRef creates a mutable reference that persists across renders without causing re-renders. Perfect for accessing DOM elements directly.

renderCycle.title: The Render Cycle
renderCycle.content: Re-render → new JSX → DOM update → useEffect runs. This cycle repeats every time state changes. Understanding it prevents infinite loops.

stateManagement.title: State Management
stateManagement.description: Compare Context, Zustand, and Jotai — see how global state flows from stores to components and triggers selective re-renders.

intro.title: Why State Management?
intro.content: When multiple components need the same data, prop drilling becomes painful. State management solutions provide a central store accessible from anywhere.

store.title: The Global Store
store.content: A global store holds application state outside the component tree. Components subscribe to it and re-render only when their slice of state changes.

approaches.title: Three Approaches
approaches.content: React offers multiple patterns: Context (built-in), Zustand (minimal store), and Jotai (atomic state). Each has different trade-offs.

context.title: Context API
context.content: Context uses a Provider component to share state. All children inside the Provider can access the value. But every consumer re-renders on any change.

zustand.title: Zustand
zustand.content: Zustand creates a store outside React. Components use useStore() with selectors to subscribe to specific slices. Only affected components re-render.

jotai.title: Jotai (Atomic)
jotai.content: Jotai splits state into independent atoms. Components subscribe to individual atoms with useAtom(). This is the most granular approach.

rerender.title: Selective Re-rendering
rerender.content: The key difference is re-render scope. Context re-renders all consumers. Zustand and Jotai only re-render components that use the changed slice.

rendering.title: Rendering Pipeline
rendering.description: Trace the journey from JSX to pixels — through createElement, Virtual DOM, Reconciliation, and DOM patches.

intro.title: How React Renders
intro.content: React doesn't manipulate the DOM directly. It uses a Virtual DOM to calculate the minimum changes needed, then applies them in a batch.

jsx.title: JSX
jsx.content: JSX looks like HTML but it's JavaScript. Babel/SWC transforms <div> into React.createElement('div') calls at build time.

createElement.title: React.createElement
createElement.content: createElement returns a plain object describing the UI element — its type, props, and children. This is a 'React Element'.

vdom.title: Virtual DOM
vdom.content: The Virtual DOM is a lightweight JavaScript tree of React Elements. It's cheap to create and compare — unlike real DOM nodes.

twoTrees.title: Two Trees
twoTrees.content: On re-render, React creates a NEW Virtual DOM tree. It now has two trees: the previous one and the new one. Time to find the differences.

diffing.title: Reconciliation (Diffing)
diffing.content: React's diffing algorithm compares both trees node-by-node. It uses 'keys' to match list items and identifies what changed, was added, or removed.

patches.title: Minimal Patches
patches.content: From the diff, React creates a minimal set of DOM operations. Instead of rebuilding everything, it only touches what actually changed.

domUpdate.title: DOM Update & Paint
domUpdate.content: React applies the patches to the real DOM in a single batch. The browser then repaints the screen. This is why React is fast — minimal DOM work.

routers.title: Routers
routers.description: Understand how client-side routing works with React Router and Tanstack Router.

intro.title: Client-Side Routing
intro.content: Single-page apps don't reload the page on navigation. A router intercepts URL changes and swaps components without a server round-trip.

url.title: URL Change
url.content: When the user clicks a link or types a URL, the router captures the change using the History API. No HTTP request is sent.

router.title: The Router
router.content: The Router component wraps your app, listening for URL changes. It's the brain that decides which component to show for each path.

routeConfig.title: Route Configuration
routeConfig.content: Routes map URL patterns to components. You define them declaratively — each <Route path='/about'> renders the About component.

pageRender.title: Page Rendering
pageRender.content: When a URL matches a route, the router renders the matched component inside an Outlet. Nested routes enable layout composition.

navigation.title: Programmatic Navigation
navigation.content: useNavigate() and <Link> allow navigation without full page reloads. Link renders an anchor tag, useNavigate works from event handlers.

fullCycle.title: The Full Cycle
fullCycle.content: Navigation → URL update → route match → component render → new navigation. This cycle creates a seamless, app-like experience.

writingCss.title: Writing CSS
writingCss.description: Compare CSS Modules, Tailwind CSS, and Panda CSS for styling React components.

intro.title: Styling in React
intro.content: React doesn't enforce a styling approach. You can use vanilla CSS, CSS Modules, utility frameworks, or CSS-in-JS solutions.

component.title: The Component
component.content: Every React component needs styles. The question is: where do styles live, how are they scoped, and how do they bundle?

approaches.title: Three Approaches
approaches.content: CSS Modules scope styles locally. Tailwind uses utility classes. Panda CSS generates type-safe, atomic CSS at build time.

cssModules.title: CSS Modules
cssModules.content: Import styles from .module.css files. Class names are automatically hashed to prevent collisions. Scoped by default, zero runtime.

tailwind.title: Tailwind CSS
tailwind.content: Compose utility classes directly in JSX. The JIT compiler generates only the CSS you use. No context switching between files.

pandaCss.title: Panda CSS
pandaCss.content: Define design tokens and recipes. Panda extracts styles at build time, generating atomic CSS with full TypeScript support.

bundle.title: Output
bundle.content: All three approaches produce optimized CSS bundles. The choice depends on team preference, project size, and type-safety needs.

componentLibraries.title: Component Libraries
componentLibraries.description: Explore Material UI, Shadcn UI, Chakra UI, and headless component primitives.

intro.title: Why Libraries?
intro.content: Building accessible, polished UI from scratch is hard. Component libraries provide battle-tested, accessible components out of the box.

app.title: Your App
app.content: Your app needs buttons, modals, forms, and navigation. You can build them yourself or use a library that handles edge cases.

compare.title: Three Approaches
compare.content: Material UI ships pre-styled components. Shadcn UI copies code into your project. Chakra UI provides runtime-styled primitives.

mui.title: Material UI
mui.content: MUI provides a ThemeProvider for global styling. Components follow Material Design guidelines. Highly customizable via the sx prop.

shadcn.title: Shadcn UI
shadcn.content: Shadcn copies component code directly into your project. Built on Radix primitives + Tailwind CSS. You own and customize every line.

chakra.title: Chakra UI
chakra.content: Chakra uses runtime CSS-in-JS with style props. Components are composable and accessible by default.

headless.title: Headless Primitives
headless.content: Radix UI and React Aria provide unstyled, accessible primitives. You bring your own styles. Maximum flexibility, zero visual opinions.

apiCalls.title: API Calls
apiCalls.description: Learn how to fetch data with Axios, React Query, SWR, and Apollo Client.

intro.title: Data Fetching
intro.content: React components need data from APIs. The challenge is managing loading states, errors, caching, and re-fetching efficiently.

component.title: The Component
component.content: A component declares what data it needs. The fetching library handles when and how to get it, and what to show while loading.

fetch.title: The Fetch Layer
fetch.content: Between your component and the API sits a data layer. It can be simple (Axios) or smart (React Query with caching).

axios.title: Axios
axios.content: Axios is an HTTP client. It handles requests, interceptors, and transforms, but YOU manage state, loading, and caching manually.

reactQuery.title: React Query / SWR
reactQuery.content: These libraries add smart caching, background re-fetching, optimistic updates, and pagination. The cache prevents redundant requests.

apollo.title: Apollo Client
apollo.content: Apollo is a GraphQL client with a normalized cache. It queries exactly the fields you need and automatically updates the UI when data changes.

api.title: The API
api.content: REST endpoints return fixed shapes. GraphQL lets clients request exactly what they need. Both work with any fetching strategy.

forms.title: Forms
forms.description: Compare React Hook Form and Formik for form management and validation.

intro.title: Form Management
intro.content: Forms are the primary way users input data. Managing values, validation, errors, and submission state gets complex quickly.

form.title: The <form>
form.content: HTML forms submit data natively. In React, we intercept onSubmit to handle data in JavaScript, enabling richer validation and UX.

compare.title: Two Libraries
compare.content: React Hook Form uses uncontrolled inputs for performance. Formik uses controlled inputs for simplicity. Both handle validation well.

rhf.title: React Hook Form
rhf.content: register() connects inputs without re-rendering on every keystroke. Uncontrolled approach means fewer re-renders and better performance.

formik.title: Formik
formik.content: Formik's <Field> components are controlled — values live in Formik's state. Simple mental model but more re-renders on large forms.

validation.title: Validation (Zod)
validation.content: Both libraries integrate with Zod for schema validation. Define your schema once and get type-safe form data automatically.

submit.title: Submission
submit.content: onSubmit receives validated data. Handle API calls, show success/error states, and reset the form — all with type safety.

testing.title: Testing
testing.description: Understand the testing pyramid with Vitest, Jest, Testing Library, Cypress, and Playwright.

intro.title: Why Testing?
intro.content: Tests give you confidence that your code works correctly. They catch bugs before users do and make refactoring safe.

code.title: Source Code
code.content: Your React app has components, hooks, and utilities. Each needs different types of tests depending on its complexity.

pyramid.title: Testing Pyramid
pyramid.content: Unit tests are fast and many. Integration tests verify components work together. E2E tests simulate real user flows — fewer but most realistic.

unit.title: Unit Tests
unit.content: Vitest and Jest run isolated tests for functions and hooks. They're blazing fast and give instant feedback during development.

integration.title: Integration Tests
integration.content: React Testing Library renders components and tests user interactions. Test behavior, not implementation details — 'click button, see result'.

e2e.title: E2E Tests
e2e.content: Cypress and Playwright launch a real browser and simulate user journeys. They catch issues that unit tests can't — network, routing, animations.

ci.title: CI/CD
ci.content: All tests run in the CI pipeline before deployment. Green tests = confident deploy. The testing pyramid ensures fast feedback with thorough coverage.

typesValidation.title: Types & Validation
typesValidation.description: See how TypeScript and Zod provide compile-time and runtime type safety.

intro.title: Type Safety
intro.content: JavaScript is dynamically typed — errors appear at runtime. TypeScript catches them at compile time. Zod validates at runtime.

js.title: JavaScript
js.content: JS lets you pass any value anywhere. This flexibility causes bugs: undefined is not a function, cannot read property of null.

ts.title: TypeScript
ts.content: TypeScript adds a type system on top of JavaScript. The compiler checks types before building — catching bugs before code runs.

types.title: Interfaces & Generics
types.content: Interfaces define object shapes. Generics (T) make functions and components reusable across types. Enums and unions restrict values.

zod.title: Zod Schemas
zod.content: Zod validates data at runtime — API responses, form inputs, environment variables. z.infer extracts TypeScript types from schemas.

checks.title: Two Layers
checks.content: TypeScript checks at compile time. Zod checks at runtime. Together they create end-to-end type safety from API to UI.

safe.title: Type-Safe App
safe.content: When your app is fully typed, refactoring is safe, autocomplete works everywhere, and bugs are caught before they reach users.

frameworks.title: Frameworks
frameworks.description: Compare Next.js, Astro, and React Router as full-stack React frameworks.

intro.title: Beyond CRA
intro.content: React itself is just a UI library. Frameworks add routing, SSR, data fetching, and build tooling to create production apps.

react.title: React App
react.content: A plain React app (Vite) is a client-side SPA. For SEO, performance, and DX, you need a framework on top.

compare.title: Three Frameworks
compare.content: Next.js is full-stack with Server Components. Astro focuses on content sites with Islands. React Router v7 adds loaders/actions.

nextjs.title: Next.js
nextjs.content: Next.js provides SSR, SSG, Server Components, and API routes. It's the most popular full-stack React framework.

astro.title: Astro
astro.content: Astro ships zero JavaScript by default. It hydrates only interactive 'islands'. Perfect for content-heavy sites with minimal interactivity.

reactRouter.title: React Router v7
reactRouter.content: React Router v7 adds data loading (loaders) and mutations (actions). It bridges the gap between SPA and full-stack.

fullstack.title: Full-Stack React
fullstack.content: Server Components and server-side rendering blur the line between frontend and backend. Choose a framework based on your app's needs.

advancedTopics.title: Advanced Topics
advancedTopics.description: Master Suspense, Error Boundaries, Portals, and Server APIs.

intro.title: Advanced Patterns
intro.content: Beyond basics, React provides powerful patterns for handling async, errors, DOM escape hatches, and server integration.

react.title: React Runtime
react.content: The React runtime manages rendering, events, and component lifecycle. Advanced APIs give you fine-grained control over these behaviors.

overview.title: Four Patterns
overview.content: Suspense handles async loading. Error Boundaries catch crashes. Portals render outside the tree. Server APIs handle mutations.

suspense.title: Suspense
suspense.content: Suspense shows a fallback while waiting for lazy-loaded components or async data. React.lazy() splits bundles automatically.

errorBoundary.title: Error Boundaries
errorBoundary.content: componentDidCatch catches rendering errors in children. Without it, one broken component crashes your entire app.

portals.title: Portals
portals.content: createPortal() renders children into a different DOM node — useful for modals, tooltips, and overlays that need to escape overflow.

serverApis.title: Server APIs
serverApis.content: React 19 adds server-side form actions and useFormState. Forms can submit directly to server functions without API endpoints.

animation.title: Animation
animation.description: Compare Framer Motion, react-spring, and GSAP for smooth UI animations.

intro.title: Why Animate?
intro.content: Animation guides attention, communicates state changes, and makes interfaces feel alive. The right library makes it easy.

trigger.title: User Action
trigger.content: Animations are triggered by user interactions, state changes, or component mount/unmount. Each library handles these differently.

compare.title: Three Libraries
compare.content: Framer Motion uses declarative props. react-spring uses physics engines. GSAP uses timeline-based sequencing.

framer.title: Framer Motion
framer.content: Declarative API: <motion.div animate={{x: 100}}>. Layout animations are automatic. Gesture handling (drag, hover, tap) built in.

spring.title: react-spring
spring.content: Physics-based: useSpring({to: {opacity: 1}}). Animations feel natural because they use spring dynamics, not fixed durations.

gsap.title: GSAP
gsap.content: Timeline-based: gsap.timeline().to(el, {x: 100}).to(el, {y: 50}). Complex choreographed sequences with precise control.

smooth.title: 60fps UI
smooth.content: All three target 60fps. Use CSS transforms (not layout properties) and requestAnimationFrame for smooth, jank-free animations.

mobile.title: Mobile Applications
mobile.description: See how React Native brings React to iOS and Android.

intro.title: React Goes Native
intro.content: React Native lets you build native mobile apps using React. Write once, run on iOS and Android — with native performance.

react.title: React Code
react.content: Your React knowledge transfers directly. Components, hooks, and state management work the same. Only the rendering target changes.

rn.title: React Native
rn.content:
React Native replaces HTML elements with native components: instead of
, instead of

, instead of .

bridge.title: Bridge & Fabric
bridge.content: The bridge (or new Fabric architecture) translates React components into native iOS UIKit and Android View calls.

platforms.title: iOS & Android
platforms.content: Each platform gets truly native UI — not a webview. Navigation, gestures, and animations use platform-native APIs.

expo.title: Expo
expo.content: Expo simplifies React Native development. No Xcode/Android Studio needed. Push updates over-the-air. Access native APIs through managed packages.

app.title: Native App
app.content: The result is a real native app: App Store and Play Store ready. Same React skills, native user experience.
